docs.title: What is mockr?
docs.text:
mockr is a CLI tool that spins up a fake REST API from a JSON config file. no backend, no setup — define your endpoints and start building.

useful when your backend isn't ready yet, or you just want to prototype something fast without spinning up a real server.

  • one JSON file, one command
  • custom status codes and response delays
  • auto-reload with watch mode
  • CORS enabled by default
  • every request logged to the terminal

No install needed

just run npx mockr-cli start in any folder with an api.json.

$ npx mockr-cli start

  mockr running on http://localhost:3000

  GET     /users
  POST    /login

docs.title: api.json format
docs.text:
Each key is a route in the format METHOD /path, each value is the response body.

{
  "GET /users": [...],
  "GET /users/1": {...},
  "POST /login": {...},
  "DELETE /users/1": { "success": true }
}

Supported methods: GET POST PUT PATCH DELETE

Note

The config file defaults to api.json in the current directory. Use --config to point to a different file.

docs.title: CLI options
docs.text:
All flags are optional.

Flag Default Description --config <file> api.json path to your config file --port <number> 3000 port to run the server on --watch off auto-reload when config changes 
mockr-cli start --config mocks/api.json --port 8080 --watch

docs.title: Response delays
docs.text:
Simulate slow networks using _delay (value in milliseconds).

{
  "GET /users": { "_delay": 1500, "data": [{ "id": 1, "name": "Alice" }] }
}

Combine with _status to simulate a slow error:

{
  "GET /slow-error": { "_delay": 2000, "_status": 503, "error": "Service Unavailable" }
}

docs.title: Basic REST API
docs.text:
{
  "GET /posts": [
    { "id": 1, "title": "Hello World", "author": "Alice" },
    { "id": 2, "title": "Second Post", "author": "Bob" }
  ],
  "GET /posts/1": { "id": 1, "title": "Hello World", "body": "...", "author": "Alice" },
  "POST /posts": { "id": 3, "message": "created" },
  "DELETE /posts/1": { "success": true }
}

docs.title: Auth flow
docs.text:
{
  "POST /login": { "token": "eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9", "expires": 3600 },
  "GET /me": { "id": 1, "name": "Alice", "email": "alice@example.com" },
  "POST /logout": { "success": true }
}

docs.title: Error handling
docs.text:
{
  "GET /admin": { "_status": 403, "error": "Forbidden" },
  "GET /timeout": { "_delay": 5000, "_status": 408, "error": "Request Timeout" },
  "GET /server-error": { "_status": 500, "error": "Something went wrong" }
}

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: mockr requires Node.js v14 or higher.

docs.title: Use without installing
docs.text:
The easiest way — no install needed:

npx mockr-cli start

docs.title: Install globally
docs.text:
Install once and run from anywhere:

npm install -g mockr-cli

Once installed, run mockr-cli start from any folder.

docs.title: Request logging
docs.text:
Every request is logged to the terminal automatically — no config needed.

  10:32:01  200  GET /users
  10:32:04  404  GET /unknown
  10:32:09  200  GET /users  (1500ms delay)
  10:32:11  403  GET /secret

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text: Up and running in under a minute.

docs.title: 1. Create api.json
docs.text:
{
  "GET /users": [{ "id": 1, "name": "Alice" }, { "id": 2, "name": "Bob" }],
  "POST /login": { "token": "abc123" }
}

docs.title: 2. Start mockr
docs.text:
npx mockr-cli start

  mockr running on http://localhost:3000

  GET     /users
  POST    /login

docs.title: 3. Fetch from your frontend
docs.text:
fetch("http://localhost:3000/users")
  .then(res => res.json())
  .then(data => console.log(data))

docs.title: Random responses
docs.text:
Use _random to return a different response each time a route is hit. useful for simulating real-world variability.

{
  "GET /status": {
    "_random": [
      { "status": "ok" },
      { "status": "degraded" },
      { "_status": 503, "error": "Service Unavailable" }
    ]
  }
}

Every request to GET /status will randomly return one of the three responses.

Tip

Combine with _delay inside each random option to simulate different response times too.

docs.title: Route params
docs.text:
Use :param syntax in your route path to match dynamic segments.

{
  "GET /users/:id": { "id": 1, "name": "Alice" }
}

When you hit GET /users/42, mockr matches the route and logs the params:

  10:32:01  200  GET /users/42  params: {"id":"42"}

The matched params are also included in the response under a params key:

{
  "id": 1,
  "name": "Alice",
  "params": { "id": "42" }
}

Note

Exact routes always take priority over param routes. GET /users/me will match before GET /users/:id if both are defined.

docs.title: Status codes
docs.text:
Return any HTTP status code using the _status field.

{
  "GET /secret": { "_status": 403, "error": "Forbidden" },
  "GET /crash":  { "_status": 500, "error": "Internal Server Error" }
}

Note

Fields starting with _ are reserved for mockr and won't appear in the response body.

docs.title: Watch mode
docs.text:
Pass --watch and mockr reloads automatically whenever you save your config file. No restart needed.

mockr-cli start --watch

  mockr running on http://localhost:3000
  watching api.json for changes...

  [reload] api.json changed

docs.title: Commands
docs.text: Command Description mockr-cli start start the mock server mockr-cli init create a starter api.json in the current folder mockr-cli update update mockr-cli to the latest version

docs.title: mockr-cli init
docs.text:
Generates a ready-to-use api.json with example routes:

npx mockr-cli init

  created api.json

  run: npx mockr-cli start

docs.title: mockr-cli update
docs.text:
Updates mockr-cli to the latest version globally:

mockr-cli update

docs.title: start options
docs.text:
All flags are optional.

Flag Default Description --config <file> api.json path to your config file --port <number> 3000 port to run the server on --watch off auto-reload when config changes 
mockr-cli start --config mocks/api.json --port 8080 --watch
